FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=10,246, 2016-2020, ages 9-14, longitudinal design with baseline and 2-year follow-up assessments

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: logistic regression analyses examining prospective associations between baseline screen time and eating disorder symptoms at two-year follow-up, plus cross-sectional associations between problematic screen use and eating disorder symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Eating and Weight Disorders - Studies on Anorexia, Bulimia and Obesity (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 MET: Qualifying phone/SM variables paired with individual-trait outcome. (1) Prospective: baseline 'social media use' (hours) → eating disorder symptoms (fear of obesity, self-worth tied to weight, compensatory behaviors, binge eating, distress); OR 1.05-1.55 reported in Table 2. (2) Prospective: baseline 'total screen time' → same eating disorder symptoms; OR 1.05-1.55. (3) Cross-sectional: 'problematic social media use' (SMAQ) → eating disorder symptoms; OR 1.26-1.82 in Table 3. (4) Cross-sectional: 'problematic mobile phone use' (MPIQ) → eating disorder symptoms; OR 1.26-1.82. All outcomes are individual-trait psychiatric/behavioral (eating disorder symptoms per KSADS-5).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: Table 2 shows ORs for screen time/social media (1.05-1.55) across five eating disorder outcomes; Table 3 shows ORs for problematic social media and mobile phone use (1.26-1.82) across five eating disorder outcomes. All p<0.05.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results throughout. Tables 2 and 3 report odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals and p-values for SM/screen-time associations with eating disorder symptoms.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies: paper contains both longitudinal analyses (baseline screen time → year-2 eating disorder symptoms) AND cross-sectional analyses (year-2 problematic screen use → year-2 eating disorder symptoms). Presence of any cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis disqualifies FT-EC7 regardless of longitudinal SM-as-DV analyses.
notes: Paper includes both longitudinal and cross-sectional designs. All SM/phone variables qualify: social media use (named platform), total screen time composite, problematic social media scale (SMAQ), problematic mobile phone scale (MPIQ).

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=8,489, longitudinal study of brain development and youth health outcomes in the United States

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multiple logistic and linear regressions examining associations between companion animal type and family involvement in sports/other activities, physical activity, and screen time

FT_IC2.evidence: No qualifying phone/SM variable. The paper analyzes pet ownership (active pets: dog/horse; passive pets: cat/fish/small animal) as predictor. Outcomes include: family involvement in sports/activities (measured by SAIQ), parent-reported physical activity (IPAQ), youth-reported physical activity (YRB), and youth screen time (STQ). Results state 'there does not appear to be a meaningful relationship between companion animal type and family involvement in sports and other activities, physical activity, or screen time.' Pet ownership is not a phone/SM variable. Screen time is the outcome in null analyses but appears only as a null finding without per-modality breakdown or SM-specific measures. FT-IC2 NOT_MET because: (1) no phone/SM variable as IV, (2) screen time only measured as outcome with null results.

notes: Paper uses ABCD data but focuses on pet ownership and family activity involvement, not phone/SM use. While screen time is mentioned, it is measured as a null outcome, not as a phone/SM IV or with phone/SM-specific breakouts.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,876 adolescents assessed annually from ages 9-12, Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal network analysis models examining connections between screen usage types and psychotic-like experiences (PLEs) across three time points (ages 9-10, 10-12, 12+ years)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Comprehensive Psychiatry (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 MET: Multiple qualifying phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait outcome (PLEs). Screen usage types measured: TV, video games, online video, social media, texting, video chat. Explicit phone/SM items: 'social media,' 'texting,' 'video chat.' Outcomes: psychotic-like experiences (hallucinations, concentration difficulties, delusions, etc.) - individual-trait psychiatric symptoms. Network analysis models show connections between screen types and PLEs. Figures 1-3 and Tables 2-4 display edge strengths linking screen usage (ScrOnlineV, ScrTV, ScrVG, ScrText, ScrChat, ScrSM) to PLE symptoms across time points. Social media and texting had highest centrality in network.

FT_IC3.evidence: Network edge weights and centrality measures reported. Table 2 (Time Point 1) shows edge strengths (e.g., ScrText-PLE_5: 0.077; ScrChat-PLE_16: 0.076; ScrSM-PLE_14: 0.012). Figures 1-3 depict network nodes and edge thickness indicating connection strength. Centrality analyses reported (expected influence, betweenness, closeness). All quantitative.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results throughout: Tables 2-4 report edge strengths (network connection weights) across three time points. Figure 4 displays expected influence, betweenness, and closeness centrality measures. Methods note LASSO algorithm used to reduce false positives and enhance accuracy. Network stability coefficient and bridge centrality measures reported.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/screen variables function as IVs (predictors) in network model, not exclusively as DVs. Longitudinal design with SM as predictor of PLEs (DV), not the reverse. EC7 applies only when SM is DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV anywhere. This paper has SM-as-IV throughout.
notes: Longitudinal network analysis comparing screen usage types (6 modalities) and PLE symptoms across three developmental time points. Two clusters identified: digital media for socializing (SM/texting/video chat) and digital media for entertainment (online video/video games/TV). Texting and online video had highest centrality. Network structure differed between ages 9-10 and 10-12 but global strength unchanged.
